diagrams.title: Z-Buffer Algorithm - Per-Pixel Depth Testing
diagrams.description: Shows how the Z-Buffer algorithm performs depth testing at each pixel. Three overlapping polygons at different depths (red=far, green=mid, blue=near) are rendered to a frame buffer and depth buffer. The diagram illustrates pixel-by-pixel comparison where the closest polygon overwrites the color buffer based on depth buffer values.
diagrams.purpose: Explains how the Z-Buffer algorithm works by storing depth at each pixel and comparing new fragments against stored depth values to determine visibility

diagrams.title: Painter's Algorithm - List Priority Rendering
diagrams.description: Demonstrates the List Priority (Painter's) algorithm where polygons are sorted by their depth (typically using farthest vertex or centroid) and rendered from farthest to nearest. The diagram shows unsorted polygons, the sorted order by depth, and the sequential overwriting process resulting in correct visibility.
elements: Three overlapping shapes at various depths
Depth calculation markers (centroid or farthest vertex)
diagrams.purpose: Explains how List Priority algorithms sort polygons by depth and render them in back-to-front order, allowing closer polygons to overwrite farther ones

diagrams.title: Z-Buffer vs List Priority - Algorithm Comparison
diagrams.description: Side-by-side comparison of both algorithms processing the same scene. Left side shows Z-Buffer with per-pixel depth testing (hardware-accelerated, handles complex intersections). Right side shows Painter's Algorithm with polygon sorting (simpler but fails with intersecting polygons). Highlights trade-offs in complexity, accuracy, and performance.
diagrams.purpose: Compares both approaches to help understand when to use Z-Buffer (modern, accurate) vs List Priority (simple scenes, sorting overhead)
